sop.coo_evaluation:
# Gaps — no SOP yet

- **Member complaint & incident log** – no documented process for recording, escalating, investigating complaints (safety issues, coach conduct, billing disputes, harassment). Critical for duty of care and pattern-spotting.
- **Financial controls & reconciliation** – no SOP for bank reconciliation, invoice approval, expense sign-off, or cash handling if applicable.
- **Staff disciplinary & performance management** – no process for documenting underperformance, coaching someone out, or formal warnings.
- **Health & safety incident response** – no SOP for injury reporting, near-misses, equipment failures, or HSE reporting thresholds.
- **Member data retention & GDPR compliance** – no documented process for data requests, deletions, or retention schedules post-cancellation.
- **Retention/win-back campaigns** – no systematic SOP for engaging at-risk members, pause-to-reactivate, or churn analysis.
- **New coach onboarding (technical)** – no SOP for gym IT access, payment setup, insurance verification, or safeguarding checks beyond initial hire.
- **Pricing & promo policy** – no SOP governing when/how discounts are offered, trial lengths, or referral mechanics.

---

# Needs updating

- **Coach performance reviews** – likely annual only; missing mid-year check-ins, member feedback loops, or trigger points for re-training.
- **Class scheduling & cover** – no logic documented on how to handle sick leave, holiday stacking, or last-minute gaps.
- **Lead qualification & trial conversion** – likely focuses on booking; missing follow-up cadence, objection handling, or why trials convert/don't.
- **Member communication (lifecycle)** – may be siloed; missing a unified journey map from inquiry → onboarding → milestone → at-risk → lapsed.

kpi.advice:
# THE FORGE — FRACTIONAL CFO ADVICE

## INCREASE REVENUE

- **Pricing power is your biggest lever.** June revenue dropped 12% month-on-month (£20.2k to £17.7k) despite member count up 21% (122 to 147). Your AR/M has collapsed to £120.48 — lowest on record and 25% below September baseline. You're under-monetising your larger membership base. Implement a price rise on new joiners immediately (£5–10/month depending on package) and move existing members to higher tiers at renewal.
- **Class utilisation is weak.** With 147 active clients in a studio now twice the size, you should be running more classes and filling off-peak slots. Revenue per member is falling, not rising post-move. Introduce peak/off-peak pricing (£2–3 discount for 10am–2pm slots) and launch a 6-class/month budget tier to capture price-sensitive members and fill quiet hours.
- **Retention crisis in recent months (5.74% churn in May, 6.60% in March, 8.33% in April).** Even June's 0% is a blip — your 3-month average is 4.1%, well above the sub-2% you achieved Sept–Nov. This is post-relocation onboarding friction. Launch a 30-day new-member check-in program and a "stay" campaign targeting members under 6 months. Recovering 5–10 members at £150/month = £750–1,500 instant revenue.
- **Upsell personal training and add-ons.** You have 147 members; PT sessions, nutrition plans, and small-group bootcamps are zero in your figures. Target 10% uptake at £25–50/session = £370–740/month minimum.

## REDUCE EXPENSES

- **Non-people costs exploded and remain 2.5× baseline.** May–June non-people spend is £9.2k–9.4k; Sept–Nov was £4.3k–4.9k. Post-relocation this should normalise — investigate: is lease/utilities higher? Are you still paying old rent? June's £9.4k against £17.7k revenue (53% of turnover) is unsustainable. Target: get this back to ≤35% of revenue (≤£6.2k by July). Call your landlord, renegotiate utilities, and audit supplier contracts immediately.
- **Payroll has drifted up 33% since September** (£2.

staff.advice:
• **Redistribute Grace's classes immediately:** Her Build: Lower (17 sessions), Build: Upper (9), Elevate (6), and Strongevity (3) must move to JoJo and Eilis. JoJo should take Build: Lower and Strongevity (stronger technical fit); Eilis takes Elevate and the remaining Build: Upper to develop her range.

• **JoJo is significantly over-loaded at 133 sessions.** Even after Grace's classes shift, she'll still be heavy. Cap her at 90–100 sessions by reducing her PT volume from 44 to 25–30 and reassigning Body Composition scans (non-coaching work) to Eilis.

• **Eilis is under-used at 48 sessions and ready for growth.** Give her Grace's Elevate classes, extra Build: Upper, and absorb JoJo's surplus PT (aiming for 12–15 new PT slots). This brings her to ~65–70 sessions and allows JoJo to step back.

• **Move all Body Composition scans (29 total across the team) to Eilis** as admin-light work. This frees up 20+ hours of JoJo's time without losing revenue, and removes a non-coaching task from the schedule.

• **No Tuesday class should lean solely on JoJo.** Confirm JoJo is still the technical lead for Tuesdays, but ensure a second coach (likely Eilis once trained) shadows or co-delivers at least one Tuesday slot monthly to build resilience and reduce single-point-of-failure risk.

• **Grace's 9 Body Composition scans** should also move to Eilis immediately—this is the fastest way to drop Grace's load to zero whilst protecting her founder time.

class_stats.suggestion:
**For quiet studio time gaps:**

1. Add a paid "Open Gym + Coach" block on Thursday and Monday evenings (your quietest days) — members pay £5-8 to use the studio with a coach available for form checks; captures those who want flexibility without a full class commitment.

2. Run a weekly 30-min "Mobility + Strength Pairing" workshop on Saturday mornings before or after your quietest slot — position it as a add-on skill-builder for Build class attendees, driving Saturday traffic and filling dead time.

**For the quieter classes themselves:**

3. Shift **Engine** and **Capacity** to Tuesday or Wednesday (your peak days) at off-peak times — test if low attendance is timing rather than interest — and send targeted invites to "Build: Full Body" attendees who've shown up 5+ times, positioning them as cardio/conditioning partners to their strength work.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Build for The Forge

**1. Map revenue-generating touch points first**
Don't start with what sounds good — list every transaction point: membership tiers, class packs, PT sessions, nutrition coaching, challenge programs, merchandise. Then reverse-engineer pricing by asking "what does a Belfast woman-focused gym charge for THIS?" (Mystery shop 3 competitors, check their Instagram pricing). This isn't theoretical — you're building a sales tool.

**2. Create two separate documents (not one)**
Coach menu = what they can actually sell/deliver + commission structure. Member menu = clean pricing with outcome hooks ("8-week transform package" not "8 PT sessions"). Coaches need to know margins and rules; members need to see value, not options paralysis. Different psychology, different formats.

**3. Build the menu around your actual capacity bottleneck**
Is it PT slots? Studio space? Coaches' availability? Price the scarcest resource higher. If you're always fully booked on evening classes but quiet mornings, tiered pricing forces shift. Use the menu to solve your operational problem, not just list offerings.

**4. Test-launch with 5 power members this week**
Don't perfect it first. Give them early access, pricing, and ask: "Would you buy this?" Gets you real feedback in days, not assumptions. This also creates early advocates who'll reference it when you go live.

**5. Publish only when coaches understand commission math**
One confused coach giving wrong pricing kills momentum. Walk through 3 real sale scenarios (6-month member, PT add-on, challenge signup) so they can pitch it naturally.

What's your current biggest revenue gap — capacity or conversion?

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Refinement - Action Plan for Grace

• **Audit your current drop-off points NOW**: Pull your analytics (Google Analytics 4) and identify which pages lose visitors fastest. Focus refinement effort on your top 3 leakiest pages first—don't rebuild everything.

• **Mine your own sales conversations**: Review the last 20 enquiries/sales calls your team handled. What questions did they ask repeatedly before buying? Those are your landing page gaps. Add a FAQ section addressing your 5 most-asked objections directly above the CTA.

• **Test headline clarity ruthlessly**: Run your current main headline past 5 women outside your target market. If they can't explain what The Forge *specifically* offers in one sentence, rewrite it. Example: Move from "Empowering Women" to "Women-Only Strength Training for Busy Belfast Professionals."

• **Competitor swipe file + differentiation**: Check 3-4 women's gyms' landing pages (even outside Belfast). Identify what clarity they're nailing, then immediately highlight what *you* do differently—exclusive female environment, specific class styles, community vibe—in above-the-fold copy.

• **A/B test one element this week**: Pick either your headline or your primary CTA button text. Change ONE thing, run it for 7 days, measure conversion lift. Even a 10% improvement compounds fast on volume.

**Quick win**: Add a 15-second explainer video on your homepage showing an actual class. Converts better than any copy.

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Implement Member Feedback System - Action Plan for Grace

• **Start with post-class captures, not forms** — Have instructors hand out 1-question cards immediately after classes (laminated, reusable). Ask: "What's ONE thing we could do better?" Collect them in a box at reception. Forms get ignored; in-the-moment feedback when endorphins are high gets honest answers. Run 4 weeks to build habit.

• **Make it frictionless for YOU to action** — Spend 15 minutes every Friday reading feedback and categorizing into: Quick Wins (can fix this week), Bigger Changes (need planning), and Noise (dismiss the outliers). Post visible results: "You asked for extended changing room hours — DONE." This closes the loop and proves you're listening, which drives retention.

• **Tie it directly to member benefits, not data collection** — Frame it as "Help shape The Forge" with a simple incentive: Every 3rd member who submits feedback gets entered into a monthly raffle for a free PT session or merchandise. Gets you 60-80% response rate instead of 10%.

• **Use feedback to hit your revenue levers immediately** — Look for patterns around: class timing gaps, equipment requests, or service pain points. If 5+ members mention childcare or locker issues, that's a pricing/service premium hiding there. Test solving it within 2 weeks, then market it to the whole member base as "by popular demand."

• **Quarterly, share wins back to the community** — Simple email or gym notice: "Your feedback = these 3 changes." Builds loyalty and social proof for new members ("We actually listen here").

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# SOP Execution Strategy for The Forge

**1. Start with your revenue-ranked services (not alphabetical order)**
Write SOPs in order of: Personal Training → Membership → Fuel Forward → Well75 → Strongevity. This hits your highest-margin/highest-touch services first, so revenue-generating gaps get fixed immediately.

**2. Use your actual member journey as the template structure**
Don't create generic SOPs. For each service, walk through: Day 1 onboarding → Week 1-4 touchpoints → Month 2+ retention → exit/upsell moments. Ask yourself: *Where do members currently get confused or drop off?* That's what the SOP needs to lock down.

**3. Get your best practitioner to narrate each SOP (then edit, don't write from scratch)**
Have your best PT deliver a session while narrating every decision they make. Record it. Same for your membership sales person during a signup. This captures the nuance you'd miss writing at a desk—then you edit for clarity and consistency.

**4. Make each SOP solve one specific problem you're currently having**
Example: If membership renewals are being missed, your SOP includes *exact email templates + trigger dates + owner responsibility*. If Well75 members don't understand progression, build that clarity into the member-facing SOP section.

**5. Assign one person as "SOP owner" per service, with quarterly review dates**
SOPs die if they're a one-time task. Schedule 15-min reviews with your PT lead, membership lead, etc. every 90 days to catch what's outdated.

What's your biggest current pain point across these services? (That's

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Content Calendar for The Forge

**Immediate action (today):**
- Use a free tool (Buffer, Later, or even a Google Sheet) to map 12 weeks ahead. Don't overthink the tool—consistency matters more than sophistication.

**Content formula that works for women's fitness:**
- **Monday/Wednesday:** Member transformation clips (30-sec before/afters, form corrections, PR celebrations)—these drive engagement AND social proof for prospects
- **Friday:** Community/lifestyle content (locker room chats, post-class coffee, member spotlights)—builds belonging
- **1 tactical post weekly:** Quick tips (nutrition, form cues, recovery), repurposed from your own expertise or member questions you hear repeatedly

**Remove the bottleneck:**
- Batch-shoot content monthly: Spend ONE Saturday filming 8-10 clips with members (consent-secured), then schedule across 4 weeks. This kills the "I forgot to post" problem.
- Assign ONE member or staff person as "content champion"—give them iPhone filming guidelines (natural light, 9:16 vertical), not a degree in videography.

**Track what actually moves needle:**
- Monitor which post types get replies + inquiries, not just likes. A post with 3 DMs asking about membership is worth 500 passive scrolls.
- Monthly 15-min review: Keep repeating what converts, ruthlessly cut what doesn't.

**Reality check:** Consistency beats perfection. An unglamorous weekly routine crushes sporadic posts.

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Videography Sessions with Jordan — Execution Plan

• **Lock in the calendar NOW (next 12 months).** Book all 6 slots with Jordan today—don't wait. Put them in both your calendars as immovable blocks. This removes decision fatigue and ensures consistency when motivation dips.

• **Define the output upfront.** Before session one, brief Jordan on exactly what you need: 3-4 short reels (30-60sec) for social, 1-2 testimonial clips, 1 behind-the-scenes piece. Specify the *angle* each time (new class launch, member transformation, facility tour). No vague "capture content" instructions.

• **Batch your filming days.** Don't film random moments across 8 weeks—block a single 3-4 hour session where Jordan films multiple scenarios back-to-back. Gets more content, costs less per piece, and keeps momentum.

• **Tag the output to your sales calendar.** Plan *when* each video drops before you film. If you're launching a new class in week 12, film that in week 10. Tie content to real business moments (referral pushes, seasonal promotions, member milestones).

• **Create a simple brief template.** Before each session, send Jordan a one-page doc: key messages, who's featured, shot list, music/tone preferences. Takes you 15 mins, saves hours of reshoots and misaligned content.

**Bottom line:** This only works if it's systematic. Make it a recurring business ritual, not an ad-hoc creative exercise.

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Systemize Automations for 6-Week Trial Enquiries — Execution Plan

• **Map the current leak first** — Before touching automations, trace exactly where trial enquirers drop off. Are they bouncing after email 1? Day 3? Never booking their first session? This tells you which automation step is actually broken, not where you *think* it is.

• **Set up a tripwire sequence, not a broadcast** — Build 3-4 triggered emails tied to *actions*, not days: (1) immediate confirmation + gym intro video, (2) if no booking within 48hrs = gentle "let's get you started" with a 1-on-1 call slot, (3) day 7 if still silent = social proof angle (success story + urgency: "spaces filling"), (4) day 10 = final "here's what you're missing" with a deadline. Each email has one job.

• **Automate the qualification gate** — Add a simple 2-question form to your trial enquiry (fitness level + goal) so automations can route beginners vs. experienced separately. One message doesn't fit all — saves money, increases conversions.

• **Lock in SMS as backup** — Email gets lost. On day 3 (if no booking), a text saying "Quick question: what's holding you back from session 1?" gets 10x higher response. It feels personal and breaks inertia.

• **Measure the *conversion* metric weekly** — Track: enquiries → booked trial → completed trial → member. If automations are working, you'll see it in that pipeline in 7-10 days, not after 6 weeks.

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Execute Role-Specific Action Checklists & KPIs for The Forge

**1. Start with outcome, work backwards to actions**
Don't assign KPIs first. For each role, ask: "What does success look like THIS quarter?" (JoJo might be: "Retain 95% of members month-on-month + onboard 12 new members." Eilis: "Zero billing errors + process 40 payments weekly." Sarah: "Deliver 15 classes with 80%+ attendance + 4.8+ ratings.") Then list the 4-5 weekly actions that directly drive that outcome. This stops busy-work creeping in.

**2. Make KPIs visible and daily, not weekly reviews**
Grace, put a shared Slack channel or Google Sheet where each person logs their metric every single day (takes 60 seconds). Weekly becomes a conversation, not a surprise. JoJo sees member cancellations same day she can fix them. Sarah spots a class bombing before next week. This is where accountability actually lives.

**3. Calibrate ambition with current reality**
Before finalising, run each person's KPI against last month's actual performance. If Eilis processed 35 payments last week, jumping to 40 isn't stretch—it's guesswork. Add 2-3 extra as the real target; write "stretch" next to it. People execute harder when they believe the number.

**4. Lock in one weekly 15-min standup per person, not a group meeting**
Each of JoJo, Eilis, Sarah gets 15 mins with you Friday afternoon. Bring the checklist, ask "What blocked you?"

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Team Vision Session Execution for Grace

**Before the session (do this NOW):**
- Write out 3-5 specific numbers/outcomes from your SSO that directly affect *their* work (e.g., "We're targeting 150 active members by Q2 — that means X new sign-ups per week your team owns"). Make it tangible, not abstract.
- Pre-assign each person's 90-day "lane" with 2-3 concrete deliverables tied to revenue/retention (not tasks). Know who owns member acquisition vs. retention vs. operations before you walk in.

**During the 60-min session:**
- Spend 10 mins on SSO context (why you're aiming for that revenue/member goal, what changed). Then pivot fast to *their* impact — "this is how your work moves the needle."
- Go person-by-person through their lane for 5-7 mins each. Say: "Your lane is X. By day 90, done looks like Y. Here's where you'll have friction — [real obstacle]. How do we solve it?" Let *them* problem-solve.
- Close with one question per person: "What do you need from me to crush this?" Write it down. Action it by EOW.

**After:**
- Send a one-pager within 24hrs: SSO, each person's lane, the 3-4 blockers you identified, and *your* commitment to unblock them. Kills ambiguity.

What's your biggest concern about alignment right now — is it clarity on the plan itself, or getting buy-in from a specific team member?

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit & Rebalance — The Forge (30 days)

• **Map actual hours, not job titles.** Have each team member log their daily tasks for 5 days (this week). You'll spot the real bottlenecks — likely someone owns member onboarding + class scheduling + social media. Don't rely on what people *say* they do.

• **Identify the 20% that's eating 80% of time.** Once you see the logs, isolate tasks that are repetitive but high-volume (e.g., WhatsApp member queries, class cancellations). These are your rebalance targets — not the skillful one-offs.

• **Task-shift before role-shift.** Don't move people around yet. Instead, move specific tasks. Example: if your lead instructor is drowning in admin, can a part-timer handle class-change notifications or member check-ins? Start there. It's faster than rewriting job descriptions.

• **Automate the drudgery first.** Before reassigning work, kill it. Use Calendly for class bookings, a simple WhatsApp chatbot for FAQs, or scheduling software for rotas. Grace's time spent automating saves 10 hours/week for staff.

• **Rebalance by week 2, not week 4.** Announce changes early in the 30 days so you catch problems (wrong person assigned, task wasn't actually removable) and adjust again before day 30. One quick pivot beats a failed rollout.

**Action today:** Request 5-day time logs from your 3 key staff members. Start Friday.

# Cost Reduction Playbook for The Forge

**Audit energy costs first** — gyms bleed money here. Get your utility bills for the last 12 months, identify peak usage times (likely mornings/evenings), then contact suppliers with this data. Switch provider or negotiate a 10-15% reduction. This alone often hits 2-3% of total costs without touching operations.

**Renegotiate supplier contracts NOW** — equipment maintenance, cleaning supplies, towel service. Call each vendor and say: "I'm consolidating suppliers to reduce spend by 10%. Match my target or I'm switching." Most will cut 5-12% rather than lose recurring revenue. Start with your biggest 3 contracts.

**Shift from full-time to hybrid staffing model** — if you're overstaffed during off-peak hours (mid-morning, early afternoons), move to part-time/zero-hours for those slots. Keep peak-time coverage strong. This typically saves 4-6% on payroll without cutting service quality.

**Cut or renegotiate your membership software/POS system** — these are negotiable. Shop competitive alternatives, get quotes, then loop back to your current provider saying you're leaving. You'll usually get 15-20% knocked off if you've got a multi-year contract they want to keep.

**Track it weekly, not monthly** — pick 2-3 biggest cost categories, assign someone 30 mins/week to monitor. Small leaks (unused subscriptions, overstocked inventory) add up fast and are easier to spot in real-time.

**Which cost category is biggest for you right now — payroll, utilities, or supplier fees?** That's where to attack

# Finance KPI Update Execution for The Forge

• **Pull actual data first, then set targets backward** — Extract last 3 months of: membership revenue, churn rate, class utilization %, and cost per member acquired. Don't guess targets; set them 8-12% above current baseline (achievable but pushes). This becomes your monthly North Star.

• **Create a one-page dashboard you check weekly, not monthly** — Monthly updates mean you're always behind. Build a simple Google Sheet with: recurring revenue (memberships × average duration), one-time revenue (class packs, merchandise), costs (staff, rent, equipment maintenance), and gross margin %. Grace checks it Mondays—catches problems early.

• **Tie KPIs directly to operational levers Grace controls** — Instead of abstract metrics, track: new member onboards (drives revenue), class attendance rate by time slot (identifies underperforming sessions to fix), and member retention at the 90-day mark (your biggest churn point). These show what to actually *do* differently.

• **Flag the one metric that's broken right now** — Assuming women's-only gyms have higher retention, if churn is creeping up, that's your diagnostic. Before next month's update, run 5 exit interviews to find out why. Fix it, then your KPIs reflect improvement.

• **Share one KPI with your team monthly** — Pick the one they influence most (likely class attendance or new member satisfaction score). Makes targets real, not just Grace's spreadsheet.

What does your current churn rate actually look like?

# Financial Forecasting Execution for The Forge

**Start here TODAY:**

• **Pull last 12 months of actual data** — membership numbers, churn rate, average revenue per member, class utilization rates, operating costs broken down (rent, staff, equipment, utilities). Stop guessing. You need the baseline before any forecast means anything.

• **Build a simple 3-scenario model** (pessimistic/realistic/optimistic) projecting 24 months ahead with these specific inputs: member acquisition rate, retention rate, pricing changes you're considering, seasonal fluctuations (January spike, summer dips), and any planned expense increases (staff hire, new equipment). Use a basic Google Sheet or Excel — nothing fancy required.

• **Identify your 3 biggest cost/revenue levers NOW** — for most gyms it's: (1) membership price/tier changes, (2) churn reduction targets, (3) ancillary revenue (PT packages, merchandise, challenges). Calculate what a 10% improvement in each does to your 24-month profit line. This tells you where to actually focus effort.

• **Set a monthly review cadence** — compare actual vs. forecast numbers every month (takes 30 mins). First month will suck; by month 3 you'll know which assumptions were garbage and adjust the model. This keeps it alive and useful instead of a document that dies.

• **Use the forecast to make ONE immediate decision** — e.g., "Can we afford that new trainer hire?" or "Should we raise prices in Q2?" Don't let this sit. Forecasts only matter if they change decisions.

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Build Your P&L Without Drowning in Spreadsheets

**Start with last month's actual data only** — don't project or estimate. Pull: revenue per service type, then reverse-engineer costs by category (instructor wages, facility overhead split by usage %, consumables). You'll spot gaps immediately (e.g., "yoga costs £X to run but generates £Y" is often a shock).

**Use the 80/20 rule to avoid analysis paralysis** — forget tracking every add-on. Focus on your 3-4 biggest revenue drivers (likely classes + 1:1 PT). Assign overhead roughly: if PT uses 30% of your floor space, it absorbs 30% of rent. Done in an afternoon.

**Set a "kill line" now** — before you build it, decide: any service below 15% margin gets cut or repriced within 60 days. Otherwise, you'll build the P&L, feel emotionally attached to every offering, and change nothing.

**Assign one person (probably you) 2 hours next Tuesday** to build this in a basic Google Sheet — rows = services, columns = revenue, direct costs, allocated overhead, margin %. Share it with your accountant for a 30-min sanity check. That's it.

**Use this quarterly, not annually** — gyms shift fast. Rerun it every 90 days. The real value is spotting *when* a class or PT package stops being profitable, not the initial snapshot.

What's your biggest revenue stream by guess right now?

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Execution Plan for Automation Audit

• **Start with your CRM data RIGHT NOW**: Export all active workflows/automations from your system (Kajabi, Pipedrive, whatever you use). Run a duplicate check by filtering for identical trigger conditions + same recipient lists. Delete dupes immediately — they're burning money on double emails and confusing your conversion metrics.

• **Map the actual enquiry→trial journey in ONE document**: Have someone (or you) physically follow a test enquiry through every touchpoint — what email lands when, what form data gets captured, where does it live, does the trial booking link actually work? Write down every step. You'll spot dead links and missing handoffs fast.

• **Test with a real scenario TODAY**: Send yourself a dummy enquiry through your website form and time exactly how long it takes to get the trial confirmation + calendar link. If it's clunky or takes >2 hours, your automation is leaking conversions.

• **Identify your REAL conversion blocker**: While auditing, flag which step loses the most people (broken link? unclear instructions? no follow-up?). Fix that ONE thing first before obsessing over perfect workflows — it'll move the needle faster than cleaning up marginal stuff.

• **Set a quarterly audit rhythm**: Make this a 90-minute task every 3 months with one team member owning it. Automations degrade; catch it early.

What CRM/automation tool are you using? That changes the specific technical steps.

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation for The Forge: Execute This Week

**1. Automate welcome sequences + member check-ins**
- Use Zapier to connect your gym booking system → email/SMS. Set it to auto-send: onboarding sequence (Days 1, 7, 30), class reminders 24h before, and "haven't seen you in 2 weeks" re-engagement prompts
- *Why this one first:* It's revenue-protecting (reduces no-shows + churn) and takes 90 minutes to set up
- *Concrete action today:* Export your member list, identify drop-off points in your current process, build 3 email templates in Mailchimp

**2. Automate class scheduling conflicts + resource requests**
- Use ChatGPT/Claude API (via Make.com) to field member requests ("Can I book a 6am spin slot?"). Configure it to check real-time capacity, suggest alternatives if full, auto-book or escalate to you only when needed
- *Saves you:* ~5-10 manual Whatsapp/DM replies daily
- *Start today:* Map out your current manual response workflow; identify the 2-3 most common member questions

**3. Automate social content repurposing**
- Use Buffer/Later to batch-create posts from one piece of content: film one 60-second member transformation clip, auto-generate 5-7 carousel posts, TikToks, and Instagram Reels variations using AI (Opus Clip or Repurpose.io)
- *ROI:* 6-8 hours of monthly content creation → 2 hours
-

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cybersecurity Execution Plan for The Forge Website

• **Audit first, don't rebuild**: Use free tools (Sucuri or MalCare) to scan your site for existing vulnerabilities *today*. This takes 30 mins and tells you what's actually broken vs. theoretical risks. Don't implement security theater—fix real gaps.

• **SSL certificate + password manager combo**: If you're not on HTTPS, get it done this week (most hosts offer free SSL now). Then enforce strong passwords for admin/staff logins using Bitwarden (free team version). This stops 80% of small gym breaches.

• **Backup automation (not manual)**: Set up automatic daily backups to cloud storage (UpdraftPlus plugin if WordPress, ~£4/month). Manual backups = forgotten backups = disaster when (not if) something goes wrong. Make it fire-and-forget.

• **Payment data isolation**: If you're taking card payments on-site, *never* store card details on the website—use Stripe or PayPal's hosted payment pages instead. One compliance requirement solved, liability dropped massively.

• **Assign one person ownership**: Give your admin/tech person a 90-minute quarterly security checklist (update plugins, review user access, test backups). Cybersecurity isn't a project—it's maintenance that prevents you losing member data and reputation.

What's your current website platform (WordPress, Wix, custom)?

marketing.advice:
**Insights**

Your cost-per-lead has exploded 4.5x in four months (£6 to £27) while volume has collapsed 94% (69 to 4 leads). This isn't seasonal noise—it's a systematic breakdown. Your close rate held at 75% in July, so the problem isn't creative or messaging fatigue; it's reach and targeting. Meta's algorithm has either throttled your audience, you're hitting saturation in a small local market, or your targeting has become too narrow. The profit margin (£458) is still healthy *per sale*, but you're now generating almost no pipeline.

Budget collapse from £435 to £109 suggests you or Meta cut spend in response to rising CPL, which is the wrong move—this created the death spiral. You're underfunded and undersaturated.

**Actions**

1. **Expand audience immediately.** You're likely trapped in a tiny lookalike or interest cohort. Test: 1% lookalike (instead of 3%), broad age range 25–55 across Belfast + 5-mile radius, and scrap interest stacking. Add a cold audience layer (website visitors 180+ days, no prior converters). You need volume back first.

2. **Resurrect April's creative and messaging angle.** April's £6/lead came from something—find that creative asset, hook, or angle and rebuild it. If you don't have records, audit what ran then vs now. Run that as a separate campaign against your expanded audience.

3. **Reset budget to £350–400/month minimum.** At £27/lead, you need 10–12 leads to hit 7–9 sales and £3,500+ revenue. You can't test or scale at £109/month. Allocate budget to 3 distinct audience cohorts: lookalike, cold website, and interest-based broad targeting—then kill the worst-performer after 2 weeks.

4. **Add a retargeting layer for warm audiences.** Your close rate is excellent; focus on traffic conversion next. Add a video view campaign (3–6 sec views) to the mix at 30% of budget—cheaper reach, warms up the market, feeds retargeting. Run
